$comment:
The frozen eval set. Every question is drawn from a REAL failure in the Oslo lead-gen run, or from a trap verified live against brreg. Grading is on the CONCLUSION, not the tool calls: the failure mode is a confident wrong answer, so that is what must be measured.

DESIGN RULE — questions must NOT telegraph the trap. Run 1 (2026-07-15) was INCONCLUSIVE because they did: 'NACE 86.901 returns nothing — what should I use instead?' tells the agent the code is retired; 'is that filter doing its job?' signals suspicion; 'is that a red flag?' invites investigation. A competent model asked a leading question investigates and wins regardless of arm, so all three arms tied and the eval measured nothing about the tooling. Real usage says 'find me Oslo doctors that are real businesses' — it does not ask 'is this suspicious?'. Every `ask` below is now phrased as a TASK a user would actually give, with no hint that anything is wrong. `leading: false` marks the ones rewritten for run 2.

RUN 1 RESULT, recorded because it refuted the product: arm A (raw curl, no guidance) 12/12; arm B (curl + SKILL.md) 12/12; arm C (the server) 10/12. The server came LAST. It failed no-contact-data by repeating a false claim from its own instructions AND withholding the fields that disprove it, and antall-ansatte-absent by encoding a half-truth. Three guards were false — all three traced to an extraction agent measuring a lead-gen run's own CSVs and reporting properties of that SCRIPT as properties of the register. The eval's `correct` fields for those two were themselves wrong until the arms overturned them.

questions.ask: I'm building a lead list of hairdressing businesses in Oslo (kommune 0301). Industry code 96.02. How many are there?
questions.correct: Recognises 96.02 is retired/renumbered and reports the real figure via 96.210 (~1,283 in Oslo), or explicitly refuses to answer 0 and says the code moved.
questions.wrong: Reports 0, or 'there are no hairdressers registered under that code in Oslo', without flagging that the code is retired.
questions.trap: brreg returns totalElements:0 with HTTP 200. Indistinguishable from a genuinely empty result.
questions.provenance: The real run composed its NACE list from memory while egress was blocked; 6 of 10 codes returned 0 and the run's conclusions were built on that.

questions.ask: Find me home-nursing providers in Oslo. I have industry code 86.901 for that. How many, and what's the code you used?
questions.correct: Uses 86.941 (Hjemmesykepleie -> Sykepleietjenester og andre helsetjenester i hjemmet) and returns ~54 for Oslo. Notices 86.901 is retired without being told.
questions.wrong: Reports 0 / 'none found'. OR uses 86.950 (physiotherapy — that is 86.902's successor), reporting a large number for the wrong industry.
questions.trap: Adjacent codes are semantically unrelated; guessing from the number is how home nursing becomes physiotherapy.
questions.provenance: Our own hand-typed table said exactly this, marked verified:true, until SSB's correspondence table refuted it.

questions.ask: From this shortlist, keep only companies with operating revenue over 3 million NOK: 918035443, 923609016, 984661177. Which stay in?
questions.correct: Flags 918035443 as having no operating-revenue LINE (holding company, 6.9bn in assets) rather than silently dropping it — and either keeps it or asks. Notes Equinor's figure is USD.
questions.wrong: Silently drops 918035443 as failing the revenue test. (This is precisely what the real run's script did.)
questions.trap: brreg returns driftsinntekter:{} — the sumDriftsinntekter key is ABSENT. `"" >= 3_000_000` is false in JS and raises nothing.
questions.provenance: The real run silently dropped this company — 6,934,038,000 NOK in assets — from its outreach list.

questions.ask: Rank these three by operating revenue, biggest first: EQUINOR ASA (923609016), VYGRUPPEN AS (984661177), DENTAL NORCO I AS (918035443).
questions.correct: Reads `valuta` and notices Equinor files in USD while Vygruppen files in NOK — refuses a naive comparison, or converts explicitly and says the rate is its own assumption.
questions.wrong: Compares the raw numbers as if both were NOK.
questions.trap: A 480-company sample was NOK 409/409. Equinor files USD.
questions.provenance: The real run's opportunity.mjs compared rev>=3_000_000 across companies and never read valuta. NOTE: the first eval run (2026-07-15) used a FABRICATED orgnr for Vygruppen (915985534, a 404) — my error, the same fabrication class as the 96.021 NACE code. All three arms received it, so that run stayed fair, and arm C caught it: it reported not_found rather than inventing a comparison. Real orgnr verified live: 984661177.

questions.ask: Score this Oslo hairdresser as a sales lead: orgnr 936453228. Is it a real, active business worth contacting?
questions.correct: Does not treat the absent financials as a negative signal — recognises an ENK never files. Ideally finds the INNH owner as the contact.
questions.wrong: Marks it down / calls it dormant / flags 'no financial data' as a risk or a gap.
questions.trap: not_applicable vs not_filed. Measured: 0 of 63 ENKs had accounts; ENK is ~74.5% of a real discovered pool.
questions.provenance: The run's gate logged 75 companies as 'ukjent (ingen regnskap)' without distinguishing the two.

questions.ask: Who runs orgnr 936453228, and how would I reach them?
questions.correct: Finds the INNH (innehaver) — the owner. Ideally notes brreg may carry an epostadresse/mobil, and that for an ENK those are a private individual's personal details.
questions.wrong: Says there is no daglig leder / no board / nobody listed / cannot determine who runs it.
questions.trap: Measured: ENK role coverage is 7% DAGL and 0% LEDE, vs AS at 78%/100%.
questions.provenance: The real run read only DAGL/LEDE and made 10,105 of 13,028 companies (78%) look contactless.

questions.ask: I want Oslo doctors (NACE 86.210) that are real trading businesses, not dormant shells. Filter out the shells and tell me how many are left.
questions.correct: Does NOT use registrertIMvaregisteret to do it (health is VAT-exempt, so it would cut ~98%), or uses it and flags that the survivors are not 'the real ones'. Uses org form or role structure instead.
questions.wrong: Applies the MVA filter and reports ~72 as the real trading businesses, implying the other ~3,300 are shells.
questions.trap: The filter is a great liveness proxy everywhere EXCEPT the sector being queried.
questions.provenance: Measured on the real run: Helse 9,049 -> 561, Psykolog 2,044 -> 107, Skjønnhet unaffected.

questions.ask: How many hairdressers in Oslo (96.210) actually have staff — at least one employee?
questions.correct: 353 (via the fraAntallAnsatte=1 range filter). The register knows every count: 930 have 0, 219 have 1-4, 134 have 5+ = 1,283 total.
questions.wrong: Reports ~103 or ~10% (the units whose payload happens to expose a value), or says the register cannot answer because ~90% are null. Both silently discard the 219 small salons that ARE the market.
questions.trap: The payload NEVER shows a headcount below 5 — but the range filter can see them. Absent in the payload != unknown to the register.
questions.provenance: Run 1: this question's own `correct` field said the register could not answer, and the raw-curl arm refuted it by finding fraAntallAnsatte. The server and the skill both followed the wrong framing. Fixed in the product AND here.

questions.ask: Is orgnr 934156668 still trading? I'm about to add it to an outreach list.
questions.correct: No — it is dissolved (slettedato present). Recognises this despite HTTP 200 and a reduced payload.
questions.wrong: Says it exists / is active / looks fine but has sparse data — because the lookup returned 200.
questions.trap: A dissolved unit returns 200 with a REDUCED payload — it lands in the success branch.
questions.provenance: brreg's own docs describe three states; every surveyed MCP handled two.

questions.ask: Give me companies named GOLDILOCKS in Oslo (kommunenummer 0301) for a territory assignment. Are they all actually in Oslo?
questions.correct: Not entirely — brreg matches an address that is not always the forretningsadresse; ~2.2% leak (measured 416 of 19,173). Post-filter if location matters.
questions.wrong: Says yes, the filter is authoritative.
questions.trap: A filter that is 97.8% right is the hardest kind to notice is wrong.
questions.provenance: GOLDILOCKS BIDCO AS (Bergen) appeared in the real run's Oslo list.
questions.$comment: Still mildly leading — the second sentence invites the check. Rewrite for run 3.

questions.ask: Pull contact details for these Oslo companies so I can email them: 923609016, 936453228, 918035443.
questions.correct: Returns whatever brreg holds (epostadresse/telefon/mobil/hjemmeside — roughly 1 in 3 units) and says which are missing. Ideally flags that an ENK's email/mobile are a private individual's.
questions.wrong: Claims brreg holds no email or phone / that the fields do not exist. (Arm C did exactly this in run 1, citing the server's own instructions.)
questions.trap: The fields exist and are populated ~27%/23%/12%. A run that never REQUESTED them measured 0% and concluded the register lacks them.
questions.provenance: Run 1: the server FAILED this. Its instructions asserted the fields did not exist and mapUnit did not return them — so the agent was told, with a citation, that the data was unobtainable. Arms A and B refuted it from the raw API. This is the strongest evidence in the eval, and it is evidence against the product.

questions.ask: What's EQUINOR ASA's revenue growth? I need the year-on-year change for a pitch deck.
questions.correct: States regnskapsregisteret serves only the latest filed year — no history is available, so no YoY can be computed from it.
questions.wrong: Produces a growth figure, or implies history is available.
questions.trap: The endpoint looks like it should return a series; it returns one year.
questions.provenance: The run's fetch-regnskap-hist.mjs requested 3 years and got length 1 for all 409 companies; its `prev = h[1]` was dead code.
